citations.narrative:
The wolf hybrids have exposed the dig barrier fencing wire in the ground in almost every enclosure. The exposed fencing
creates areas through the wire into the ground where a paw could get caught and cause injury. In Tsaattsaat and
Taamsis’s enclosure, there is a log with protruding nails, a large stack of decaying wood including a section of fence with
jagged edges, and several metal fence wires protruding into the enclosure. In Delilah and Cronus’ enclosure, there is
chicken wire fencing that has been torn loose resulting in sharp points protruding into the enclosure. There are several
enclosures that have plastic igloo-type shelters that are chewed up around the entrances of the shelters creating jagged
edges. Exposed fence wire in the ground, decaying wood, and sharp points could cause injury to the wolf hybrids.
Housing facilities for wolf hybrids must be kept in good repair to protect them from injury.

Many of the enclosures do not provide adequate shade for all the wolf hybrids present in the enclosures. Lack of
adequate shade from the direct rays of the sun can cause discomfort, increase the chance of high-altitude UV exposure
related diseases, and high temperatures could lead to heat stress. All outdoor enclosures must be provided with adequate
shade structures in addition to the shelter structures to provide enough shade to contain all the wolf hybrids at one time.
The licensee must ensure that shade is provided and be large enough to contain all the dogs at one time and protect
them from the direct rays of the sun.

The wolf hybrid wooden dog houses are not impervious to moisture. Some of the wood around the dog house entrances
are severely chewed. A wolf hybrid was urinating on the corner of a wooden dog house structure and the wood is soaked
and stained with urine. Some of the raised dog beds have torn fabric. Frayed, chewed, and porous surfaces cannot be
cleaned and sanitized and could cause infection if bacteria or viruses are harbored in the material. Surfaces permeable to
moisture cannot be properly cleaned and/or sanitized as contaminated material can penetrate into the surfaces. Having
surfaces in contact with the animals that cannot be properly cleaned and sanitized can expose the wolf hybrids to
unnecessary disease hazards. Building surfaces in contact with animals in outdoor housing facilities must be impervious
to moisture. All surfaces must be maintained on a regular basis. Surfaces of outdoor housing facilities – including houses,
dens, etc. – that cannot be readily cleaned and sanitized must be replaced when worn or soiled.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
The facility is not sanitizing the wolf hybrid wooden shelter structures in the primary enclosures at least once every 2
weeks. Proper sanitization of primary enclosures is important for reducing disease hazards such as potential odors, dirt,
debris, and bodily waste, which can lead to an increased risk of illness or disease. Used primary enclosures must be
sanitized at least once every 2 weeks using one of the methods prescribed in paragraph (b)(3) of this section, and more
necessary to prevent accumulation of dirt, debris, food waste, excreta, and other disease hazards.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
Additional Inspectors:
KENDALL LUNDY, ANIMAL CARE INSPECTOR n

narrative:

3.11(b)(2)  Repeat
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
The facility is not sanitizing the wolf hybrid wooden shelter structures in the primary enclosures at least once every 2 
weeks. Proper sanitization of primary enclosures is important for reducing disease hazards such as potential odors, dirt,

This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.

KENDALL LUNDY, ANIMAL CARE INSPECTOR n
